nativeRange.habitat: Small tributaries, creeks, pools, and seasonally flooded forest areas with slow-moving, warm water

diet.notes: Readily accepts sinking pellets and wafers as a staple, and benefits from supplementation with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and Artemia. The Sterbai Cory scavenges the bottom of the tank, so sinking foods should be offered at substrate level to ensure it receives adequate nutrition.

summary: The Sterbai Cory (Corydoras sterbai) is a freshwater catfish from South America prized for its bold spotted pattern and vivid orange fins. It thrives at warmer temperatures than most cories, making it well-suited as a bottom-dweller in Discus and Ram tanks. Best kept in groups of six or more on fine sand.
careNotes: The Sterbai Cory is commonly considered one of the most popular Corydoras species in the hobby, distinguished by its bold spotted and reticulated pattern and orange-hued pectoral fins. Unlike most Corydoras, it tends to tolerate and even prefer warmer water in the range of 24–30°C, making it well-suited for tanks housing Discus or German Blue Rams — species that typically require elevated temperatures. An albino variant is available in the trade and is generally regarded as hardier than many albino morphs of other catfish. A fine sand substrate is strongly preferred over gravel; soft substrate protects the sensitive barbels (the whisker-like sensory appendages around the mouth) from abrasion, injury, and infection. Undergravel filters are generally considered incompatible with this species, as the flow they draw through the substrate can damage the barbels and lead to infections. Copper-based medications have been reported to be harmful to this species and should be avoided; alternative treatments should be sought when disease arises. Like all Corydoras, the Sterbai Cory is a facultative air-breather — individuals will periodically dart to the surface to gulp air, which is a normal behavior rather than a sign of distress. Paraguay has been cited as an additional part of the range by some sources, though the core documented range is the Rio Guaporé basin in Brazil and Bolivia. The older scientific name Hoplisoma sterbai is a valid synonym recognized in some taxonomic databases, though Corydoras sterbai remains the name almost universally used in the aquarium trade.
breedingNotes: The Sterbai Cory is generally considered one of the more accessible Corydoras species to spawn in captivity. Like other members of the genus, they are egg-scatterers that deposit adhesive eggs on flat surfaces and plant leaves. Breeding tends to be triggered by conditions that mimic the onset of the rainy season — cool partial water changes, increased feeding of live or frozen foods, and slightly elevated temperatures are commonly reported triggers. Warmer water in the range of 26–28°C is typically maintained during the conditioning period. Fry are small and benefit from infusoria or micro-worm as first foods. While pairs and groups will often spawn with relatively little intervention, raising fry successfully requires close attention to water quality and ensuring appropriately sized food is available from the start.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary species profile. Provided size (6.5 cm), pH (6.0–7.6), hardness (1–15°H), native range (Bolivia/Brazil, Rio Guaporé), habitat description, min tank dimensions (45×30×30 cm), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (also listed as Hoplisoma sterbai), taxonomy (Callichthyidae / Siluriformes), max size (6.8 cm SL), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dH), and native range (central Brazil and Bolivia). FishBase temperature of 21–25°C reflects cooler wild habitat data; aquarium-hobby sources consistently recommend 24°C minimum for captive care, and upper bound of 30°C supported by warm-water association with Discus tanks.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed warm-water tolerance and Discus/Ram compatibility. General corydoras range given as 72–82°F (22–28°C); sterbai specifically noted as tolerating higher temperatures. Also confirms group-of-six recommendation and sinking-food diet. pH given as 6.6–8.0 (broad trade range).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers that deposit adhesive eggs on flat surfaces and plants. Breeding is commonly triggered by cool partial water changes combined with increased live or frozen food feeding. Fry are small and require infusoria or micro-worm as first foods.
